name: Nelarabine
description: Nelarabine is a chemotherapy drug used in T-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia. Nelarabine is a purine nucleoside analog converted to its corresponding arabinosylguanine nucleotide triphosphate (araGTP), resulting in inhibition of DNA synthesis and cytotoxicity.
indication: For the treatment of pediatric and adult patients with acute T-cell lymphoblastic leukemia and T-cell lymphoblastic lymphoma whose disease has not responded to or has relapsed following treatment with at least two chemotherapy regimens.
toxicity: A single IV dose of 4,800 mg/m^2 was lethal in monkeys, and was associated with CNS signs including reduced/shallow respiration, reduced reflexes, and flaccid muscle tone. It is anticipated that overdosage would result in severe neurotoxicity (possibly including paralysis, coma), myelosuppression, and potentially death.
mechanismOfAction: Once nelarabine is metabolized into ara-GTP, the metabolite accumulates in leukemic blasts and incorporates into DNA to exert its S phase-specific cytotoxic effects, leading to the induction of fragmentation and apoptosis. Ara-GTP competes with endogenous deoxyGTP (dGTP) for incorporation into DNA. Once ara-GTP is incorporated at the 3' end of DNA, further DNA elongation is inhibited, which signals apoptosis and leads to cellular destruction. Additional cytotoxic activities may exist, but these are not fully understood.
pharmacodynamics: Nelarabine is a prodrug of the cytotoxic deoxyguanosine analogue 9-ß-D-arabinofuranosylguanine (ara-G). Nelarabine is demethylated by adenosine deaminase (ADA) to ara-G. Ara-G is then transported into cells, where it undergoes three phosphorylation steps, resulting in the formation of ara-G triphosphate (ara-GTP). In the first phosphorylation step, ara-G is converted to ara-G monophosphate (ara-GMP). Ara-GMP is then monophosphorylated by deoxyguanosine kinase and deoxycytidine kinase to ara-G diphosphate, and then subsequently to the active ara-G triphosphate (ara-GTP). Ara-GTP is the one that exerts the pharmacological effect. Pre-clinical studies suggest that T-cells are particularly sensitive to nelarabine.

interactions.name: Pentostatin
interactions.description: Pentostatin may decrease the antineoplastic activities of Nelarabine.

interactions.name: Metamizole
interactions.description: The risk or severity of myelosuppression can be increased when Metamizole is combined with Nelarabine.

interactions.name: Natalizumab
interactions.description: The risk or severity of adverse effects can be increased when Nelarabine is combined with Natalizumab.

interactions.name: Pimecrolimus
interactions.description: The risk or severity of adverse effects can be increased when Pimecrolimus is combined with Nelarabine.

interactions.name: Tofacitinib
interactions.description: Nelarabine may increase the immunosuppressive activities of Tofacitinib.

interactions.name: Tacrolimus
interactions.description: The risk or severity of adverse effects can be increased when Tacrolimus is combined with Nelarabine.

interactions.name: Clozapine
interactions.description: The risk or severity of adverse effects can be increased when Nelarabine is combined with Clozapine.

interactions.name: BCG vaccine
interactions.description: The therapeutic efficacy of BCG vaccine can be decreased when used in combination with Nelarabine.

interactions.name: Rabies virus inactivated antigen, A
interactions.description: The risk or severity of adverse effects can be increased when Nelarabine is combined with Rabies virus inactivated antigen, A.

interactions.name: Trastuzumab
interactions.description: Trastuzumab may increase the cardiotoxic activities of Nelarabine.

interactions.name: Leflunomide
interactions.description: The risk or severity of adverse effects can be increased when Nelarabine is combined with Leflunomide.
